content.content:
=== add-rig-list-rig-show-tools-to-the/decisions.yaml :: D8
Q: Should rig-show accept a rig id or a writ id as its lookup key?
Rec: c
Selected: custom
Override: rig-show accepts rig only, but create a separate tool and API helper to retrieve 0-1 rigs for a writId
Stakes: n/a

=== anima-session-generic-engine/decisions.yaml :: D6
Q: What is the cwd resolution chain when givens.cwd is missing?
Rec: a
Selected: custom
Override: Do not provide a fallback. Throw if givens.cwd is undefined or missing.
Stakes: high

=== astrolabe-mvp-part-1/decisions.yaml :: D22
Q: What permissions should the astrolabe.sage role grant?
Rec: a
Selected: custom
Override: ['astrolabe:read', 'astrolabe:write', 'clerk:read'] — read/write plans + read writs; posting of final mandate is done by an engine
Stakes: high

=== astrolabe-mvp-part-1/decisions.yaml :: D23
Q: Should the sage role carry inline instructions or an instructionsFile?
Rec: c
Selected: custom
Override: instructionsFile — point to a markdown file in the package; include minimal placeholder for content now, which will be added to later
Stakes: low

=== astrolabe-mvp-part-1/decisions.yaml :: D36
Q: How should the planId be delivered to anima-session engines, given that inline string interpolation is not yet available in the Spider?
Rec: a
Selected: custom
Override: use inline string interpolation -- it has been developed in the most recent version
Stakes: high

=== astrolabe-mvp-part-1/decisions.yaml :: D43
Q: Should plan-init yield prompt strings for all three stages, or should prompts be static in the template?
Rec: a
Selected: custom
Override: prompts are defined as templates on each engine, using the `${yields.plan-init.<id>}` syntax to get the plan id
Stakes: low

=== brief-oculus-page-for-patron/decisions.yaml :: D21
Q: When should text answers auto-save?
Rec: a

=== cancellable-animator-sessions/decisions.yaml :: D3

content.text:
# Plandoc mining results — 35 overrides across 38 specs

Striking baseline result first: **24 of 38 specs had zero patron overrides** (63% of briefs sail through as planned). The planner is already right most of the time. The override signal is about **where it's systematically wrong**.

## The Numbers

- 38 specs reviewed, ~945 total decisions
- 35 patron overrides → **~3.7% override rate**
- 14 specs had at least one override (37% of specs)
- Hot spots: `writ-parentchild` (5 overrides), `astrolabe-mvp-part-1` (4), `parlour-page-for-oculus` (4), `configurable-rig-templates` (4)

## Pattern Clusters (the actual findings)

I coded all 35 overrides. Six clusters emerged — and **three of them are really policy preferences masquerading as per-decision overrides**, meaning they can be codified as defaults.

### Cluster A — Cutline policy (9 overrides). CODIFIABLE.
The planner defaults conservative: keep the old path, add a deprecation window, provide a fallback-when-missing. Sean repeatedly says **"rip it out, fail loud."**

- `fix-startup D12` — no deprecation period, just remove
- `configurable-rig-templates D26` — remove existing static engine config entirely, don't dual-path
- `configurable-rig-templates D3` — no hardcoded fallback, fail if no match
- `configurable-rig-templates D11` — "Disregard brief directive" — no special `$role` variable
- `spider-support-givensspec D24/D25` — remove bare-`$` pattern support entirely
- `anima-session D6` — throw instead of fallback
- `doc-update-pass D7/D10` — remove `_agent-context.md` entirely, don't update it
- `writ-parentchild D41` — don't denormalize

**Rule to codify:** *Prefer removal to deprecation. Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback. Don't keep two systems; pick one.* Decisions that reduce to "keep old path vs. rip it out" default to rip-it-out.

### Cluster B — "Build the API properly" (5 overrides). CODIFIABLE.
When the planner's recommendation involves a hack, a workaround, or "the anima will figure it out," Sean says **add the first-class API method and the matching tools**.

- `parlour-page D15` — Extend `LoomApi` with `listRoles()` + create `role-list`, `role-show` tools
- `spider-page D17` — Add `listEngineDesigns` to `FabricatorApi` + matching tools
- `spider-page D18` — Add `listBlockTypes` to `SpiderApi` + matching tools
- `astrolabe-mvp D22` — Don't grant `clerk:write` to the anima; have an engine post the mandate

**Rule to codify:** *If the recommendation requires a workaround, clever routing, or "the anima will handle it via prompt," default to extending the API at the right layer and calling it directly.*

### Cluster C — Stale planner knowledge (3 overrides). DIAGNOSIS, not razor.
The planner worked from an outdated mental model of what's built.

- `astrolabe-mvp D36` — "inline string interpolation has been developed in the most recent version"
- `astrolabe-mvp D43` — downstream of D36
- `configurable-rig-templates D11` — brief itself was stale

**Not a razor criterion.** This is the **atlas staleness** problem (click `c-mo1mqhes` cluster). If the planner had a fresh "what landed recently" view, these wouldn't surface.

### Cluster D — UX & interaction specifics (5 overrides). SURFACE.
Genuinely value-laden. Planner cannot discover this by reading code.

- `brief-oculus D21` — auto-save on change, with debounce timer
- `brief-oculus D25` — specific radio behavior for custom input
- `parlour-page D19` — title = topic-or-first-message-truncated
- `parlour-page D23` — cost card position (sidebar bottom)
- `parlour-page D30` — codex dropdown + lazy worktree creation

**Confirms Razor Criterion #2** from my earlier pass (*human-facing surface*). This is the cleanest cluster — these are exactly the decisions a planner can't make.

### Cluster E — Responsibility assignment (6 overrides). SURFACE.
Who owns the behavior — engine vs tool vs apparatus-start vs closure. These involve architectural taste about component boundaries.

- `astrolabe-mvp D22` — engine owns the write, not the anima
- `astrolabe-mvp D23` — role has instructionsFile (placeholder now) — set up the hook
- `astrolabe-mvp D43` — prompts defined as templates on each engine
- `fix-startup D23` — walk up to parent apparatus for requires/recommends
- `writ-parentchild D18` — fail parent only, let existing cascade handle siblings
- `writ-parentchild D43` — specific cancellation text

**Overlaps with Razor Criterion #1** (*establishes pattern*). When a decision sets the *pattern for who owns what*, it should surface.

### Cluster F — Data shape / abstraction level (5 overrides). SURFACE, but narrowly.
Type ergonomics and abstraction level.

- `cancellable-animator D3` — `Record<string, unknown>` opaque blob (less-typed than planner proposed)
- `writ-parentchild D3` — specific optional/required split
- `writ-parentchild D28` — what `writ-show` returns
- `loom-charter D1` — convention-based path resolution
- `loom-charter D3` — convention only, no explicit config

**New Razor Criterion candidate:** *abstraction level and shape of persisted data* — especially when it's the shape of something other components will consume. Planner tends to over-type or over-configure.

## Updated Razor (synthesizing both passes)

### Surface for ratification (value-laden)
1. **Vocabulary/pattern establishment** — introducing new terms, new categorical distinctions (confirmed; matches cluster E)
2. **Human-facing surface** — CLI, UX, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing (confirmed strongly; cluster D)
3. **Scope boundary** — "should we also do X?" beyond commission intent (weakly confirmed)
4. **Shape of persisted/inter-component data** — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention (cluster F — new)
5. **Component responsibility boundaries** — when a decision sets *who owns* a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses (cluster E — new, and probably the biggest miss in my first pass)

### Pick + record (non-value-laden)
- Follow-the-pattern decisions (Clerk/Spider/Fabricator conventions) — **the planner is ~100% right on these when a pattern exists**
- Module structure, test file layout, Zod schema shapes
- Naming when a convention exists
- Architecture-spec-prescribed choices (baseline was "follow the spec" and Sean usually agreed)

### Codify as defaults (no decision needed; just do it)
- **Prefer removal to deprecation** — no "keep the old path as fallback" unless patron explicitly asks
- **Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback** — throw when input is missing, don't default
- **One system, not two** — when refactoring, don't preserve the old path alongside the new
- **Extend the API at the right layer, don't route around it** — if recommendation involves workaround or "the anima handles it via prompt," add the method/tool instead

## The Three Big Takeaways

**1. The override rate is already low (~3.7%).** The planner is doing well. What we're optimizing is a narrow exception path, not a bulk problem.

**2. ~40% of overrides (clusters A + B, 14 of 35) are patron *policy preferences* the planner hasn't been told — not per-decision value judgments.** Codifying these as default rules in the sage-writer instructions would drop the override rate to ~2% without expanding the surfaced-decision set. This is higher-leverage than building a preference-learning model; it's just encoding the preferences Sean has already expressed.

**3. Cluster C (stale knowledge) is a separate problem.** Atlas freshness. Not a razor issue — it's the planner working from yesterday's codebase. Connects directly to the atlas cluster `c-mo1mqhes`.

## Recommendation

Three concrete moves:

1. **Update the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions with the three codifiable defaults** (removal-over-deprecation, fail-loud-over-fallback, extend-API-over-hack). Direct instruction update, not a commission. Expected effect: ~40% reduction in overrides.

2. **Replace `c-mo1mq9qa` with a tighter click** — *"Define the razor for value-laden decisions"* — with the 5 criteria above (vocabulary, human-facing, scope, data-shape, responsibility). Drop the tier language entirely.

3. **Drop/park `c-mo1mq9f6`** (preference model) as not-MVP. The override data shows a preference model wouldn't help much — most overrides are already-expressible policy, not per-decision patron taste that needs learning.

Want me to draft the three codified-default rules in the sage-writer instruction format so you can see what they'd look like in place?

content: We're redesigning the Astrolabe planner to be more opinionated so fewer decisions surface for patron review, and just finished mining 35 real plandoc overrides to sharpen the razor. Next: draft the three codified default rules for sage-writer instructions. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
# Preview — clicks to distill from this analysis

Here are the questions this work answered, framed as clicks. **Five clicks total**: two conclude existing live clicks, three are new+concluded. All hang under `c-mo1mq93f` (planner redesign umbrella).

## Conclude existing (2)

### 1. `c-mo1mq9qa` — *"Design tiered decision model that proceeds on best-guess defaults"*
**Conclusion:** Tier model scrapped. Replaced by a binary pick+record / surface split with a razor (see new click "Razor for value-laden planner decisions"). The 4-tier framing presumed Tier 1–3 plumbing distinctions would be useful MVP design; empirical override analysis of 35 overrides across 38 specs showed (a) the pick/surface binary is the only edge that matters at MVP, and (b) ~40% of what looked like Tier 4 value-judgments are actually codifiable policy preferences Sean has already expressed — not per-decision taste. Tiered plumbing can be designed later if concrete decisions reveal the need.

### 2. `c-mo1mq9f6` — *"Design the preference model that graduates patron corrections into planning defaults"*
**Conclusion (dropped/deferred):** Not MVP. Override-rate analysis shows the manual learning loop (patron reads records, updates sage instructions) is already operational; automating correction-graduation offers diminishing returns versus simply codifying Sean's already-expressed policy preferences (see "Codifiable policy defaults" click). Revisit only if override rate plateaus above a threshold that manual codification can't drive down.

## New concluded clicks (3)

### N1. *"What razor identifies value-laden planner decisions that should surface to the patron?"*
**Parent:** `c-mo1mq93f`
**Conclusion:** Five criteria, derived from mining 35 real patron overrides across 38 specs. A decision surfaces if and only if it is:
1. **Vocabulary/pattern establishment** — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow.
2. **Human-facing surface** — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details.
3. **Scope boundary** — cutlines between commission and follow-up; "should we also do X?" questions.
4. **Shape of persisted or inter-component data** — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape.
5. **Component responsibility boundaries** — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership.

Everything else: pick (confidently or arbitrary-with-flag) + record. Uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it's a planner bug (read more code).

### N2. *"What codifiable default rules would reduce the planner's override rate without expanding patron surfacing?"*
**Parent:** `c-mo1mq93f`
**Conclusion:** Three rules, accounting for ~40% of observed overrides (clusters A+B from the override analysis):
1. **Prefer removal to deprecation.** When refactoring, rip out the old path. No deprecation windows unless patron explicitly asks.
2. **Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback.** Throw on missing input; no defaults-when-absent unless the absent case is a legitimate state.
3. **Extend the API at the right layer; don't route around it.** If the recommendation involves a workaround or "the anima handles it via prompt," default to adding the method/tool instead.

These are Sean's architectural taste expressed as standing rules. Codifying them in the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions is the next operational step.

### N3. *"What is the empirical patron-override rate on current Astrolabe planner output?"*
**Parent:** `c-mo1mq93f`
**Conclusion:** Baseline measurement as of 2026-04-16: **~3.7% override rate** (35 overrides / ~945 total decisions across 38 specs). 24 specs (63%) had zero overrides; 14 (37%) had at least one. Hot spots: `writ-parentchild-relationships` (5), `astrolabe-mvp-part-1` (4), `parlour-page-for-oculus` (4), `configurable-rig-templates-via-guild` (4). The planner is already correct on the bulk of decisions when a codebase pattern, architecture spec, or clear technical constraint exists. Future work targets the narrow exception path, not bulk quality. Re-measure after razor + codified defaults ship.

## Cross-link, not new click

**Cluster C (stale planner knowledge — 3 overrides)** is addressed by the atlas-staleness cluster `c-mo1mqhes`. I'd add an outbound `related` link from N1 → `c-mo1mqhes` with a note: "stale-knowledge overrides are atlas-freshness problems, not razor-criterion gaps."

## Umbrella goal edit

`c-mo1mq93f`'s goal currently says *"...with **tiered** decision model."* Propose editing to *"...with **binary razor for surfacing decisions**."* — the tier language is now superseded. Not concluding the umbrella (still has open children: handoff notes).

## What's NOT captured as clicks

Deliberately leaving these as operational follow-ups rather than clicks:
- Drafting the actual sage-writer instruction updates (direct instruction edit, not a decision)
- The format of the decisions record in the spec (minor, can be settled when we ship N2)
- Handoff notes (`c-mo1mqa1y` is already open for that)

---

**Questions before I create:**
1. Do N1 and N2 feel like the right granularity, or should they merge into one "Planner decision policy" click? I lean separate — surfacing vs. silent-defaulting are conceptually distinct — but you might want one.
2. For N3 (override rate), should I also stash the raw extracted override data (the 35-row list) as an artifact, or is the click body enough?
3. Any razor criterion wording you want tightened before I commit?

content.text:
# Done ✓

All 5 click changes plus the cross-link are in place. Click state is stored in the ratchet database (not git), so nothing to commit.

## Summary

| Action | Click | ID |
|---|---|---|
| Concluded | Tier model design | `c-mo1mq9qa` |
| Dropped | Preference model design | `c-mo1mq9f6` |
| Created + concluded | Razor for value-laden planner decisions | `c-mo1yb8aj` |
| Created + concluded | Codifiable policy defaults from override analysis | `c-mo1yb8nf` |
| Created + concluded | Empirical patron-override rate baseline | `c-mo1yb8zb` |
| Linked (related) | Razor → Cut Astrolabe per-commission cost | `c-mo1yb8aj → c-mo1mqhes` |

**Planner-redesign umbrella `c-mo1mq93f` now shows:** 8 concluded children, 4 dropped, 1 live (handoff notes `c-mo1mqa1y` — the only remaining open question under the umbrella).

## One snag — umbrella goal edit

I wanted to edit `c-mo1mq93f`'s goal text from *"...with **tiered** decision model"* to *"...with **binary razor for surfacing decisions**"*. **The `nsg click` CLI doesn't expose an edit/update command** — only create, show, list, park, resume, conclude, drop, reparent, link, unlink, extract, tree.

Options:
1. Leave the stale phrasing. The conclusion on `c-mo1mq9qa` documents the scrap, so anyone reading the umbrella's tree will see the corrected picture downstream. Low-cost to ignore.
2. Add a click-editing subcommand to the CLI (small commission or direct work). Probably worth doing generally — goal drift is inevitable.
3. Drop the umbrella and recreate with correct goal text, migrating children. Heavy-handed and breaks history.

I'd recommend #1 for now and file #2 as a separate improvement if it bothers you. Want me to capture #2 as a click under something like an "nsg CLI ergonomics" umbrella, or add it to the backlog?

input.description: Show the policy conclusion click about switching back to single-session

content.content:
{
  "id": "c-mo1xoggo-976221bef8a5",
  "goal": "Policy: change default implement engine from implement-loop back to implement (single-session) given X010 H1 findings",
  "status": "concluded",
  "createdAt": "2026-04-16T20:30:20.760Z",
  "parentId": "c-mo1vzt8c-417c2ea3bc17",
  "conclusion": "Change the default `rigTemplates.default.engines[1].designId` from `implement-loop` back to `implement` (single-session).

## Rationale

X010 H1 empirical testing falsified the cheap-mining projection that decomposition reduces cost. The Oculus click-page mandate run under both engines produced:

| Metric | Monolithic (`implement`) | Decomposed (`implement-loop`) | mono/decomp |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| Turns | 77 | 267 | 0.29x |
| Wall time | 12.5 min | 45 min | 0.28x |
| Billed cost (impl) | $7.07 | $18.58 | 0.38x |
| Review | PASS | PASS | tie |
| Seal | fast-forward | rebase conflict (stuck) | mono wins |

See `experiments/X010-staged-sessions/artifacts/2026-04-16-h1-monolithic-baseline.md` for the full analysis.

## Quality comparison (side-by-side)

Merged monolithic (`eb09729`+`be7cbc4` on origin/main) vs stuck decomposed (`draft-mo1o66gw-d09ff833`) on the same spec:

- Feature parity: Both implement all 25 decisions (D1-D25). Both passed independent review.
- Code volume: Mono `index.html` is 1,043 lines; decomposed is 1,441 (+38%). Decomposed is more fragmented into small named helpers (`renderLifecycleSection`, `renderLinksSection`, etc.); mono keeps rendering more unified.
- Test coverage: Mono has 58 tests (35 tree + 23 detail); decomposed has 69 (40 + 29) -- about +19%. Coverage topics overlap almost entirely. Decomposed has a couple of marginal extras (e.g., explicit "missing id not in forest" breadcrumb path, non-string coercion in a couple helpers).
- Observable behavior differences: Essentially none. Both render the same tree, detail pane, breadcrumb, link management, status filters, lifecycle controls. Decomposed's breadcrumb surfaces a "(not found)" affordance when scoping to a missing id; mono just hides the breadcrumb.

No quality premium observed that would justify decomposition's 2.6x cost and 3.6x wall-time premium. If anything, the monolithic output is more compact for the same feature set.

## Decision

Keep `implement` (single-session) as the default engine. `implement-loop` with piece decomposition remains available but should be a deliberate per-commission opt-in, not the default. Revisit if H4 testing at much larger scales (500+ turns monolithic) surfaces a threshold where decomposition pays off.

## Action taken

`guild.json` at /workspace/vibers/guild.json already has `engines[1].designId: implement` from the baseline rig run -- leave it as-is rather than reverting.",

content.text:
Done. Three clicks dropped (`c-mo1mqa1y`, `c-mo1mqadm`, `c-mo1mqap7`), all with conclusions pointing to `c-mo1xoggo` and cross-referencing `c-mo1w11jq` as the surviving home if decomposition ever revives.

## Follow-up question — should we conclude the umbrella?

`c-mo1mq93f` (planner redesign umbrella) **no longer has any live children** after this drop:

- 4 original decisions concluded (intent vs prescription, planner info, tier resolution, ratify after)
- 3 originally dropped (seam contracts, task type, terminal verify)
- 3 new concluded today (razor, codified defaults, baseline)
- 2 dropped today (tier model scrapped, preference model deferred)
- 3 dropped today (handoff notes trio)

The only work remaining under this umbrella is **operational** — updating the Astrolabe sage-writer instructions with the razor criteria and the three codified defaults. That's a direct instruction edit, not a design click.

Options:
1. **Conclude `c-mo1mq93f`** — its design questions are all answered; the goal text will also stop drifting since immutable goals can't be re-edited anyway.
2. **Leave it live** until the sage-writer instruction updates are actually shipped, so it shows as "in flight" not "designed."
3. **Leave it live** and let the instruction-update work spawn a child click when you start it.

I'd lean #1 — the design is closed, operational work is separately tracked in the coco-log when we do it. But you know the sage-writer update timeline better. Which do you want?

content.content:
Usage: nsg [options] [command]

Nexus Mk 2.1 — guild CLI

Options:
  --guild-root <path>                 Guild root directory (default: $GUILD_ROOT
                                      or auto-detect from cwd)
  -h, --help                          display help for command

Commands:
  init [options]                      Create a new guild — directory structure,
                                      guild.json, and package.json
  version [options]                   Show Nexus framework and installed plugin
                                      version information
  status [options]                    Show guild identity and installed plugin
                                      summary
  upgrade [options]                   Upgrade the guild framework and run
                                      pending plugin migrations
  start [options]                     Start the guild daemon (tool server +
                                      oculus + spider crawl loop)
  stop [options]                      Stop the guild daemon (graceful SIGTERM
                                      with SIGKILL escalation)
  plugin                              plugin commands
  session                             session commands
  summon [options]                    Summon an anima — compose context and
                                      launch a session
  commission-post [options]           Post a new commission, creating a writ in
                                      open or new (draft) status
  piece-add [options]                 Add a new piece (atomic task) to a mandate
                                      for sequential execution
  writ                                writ commands
  plan                                plan commands
  inventory-write [options]           Write the codebase inventory for a plan
  scope-write [options] [planId]      Write or replace the scope items for a
                                      plan
  decisions-write [options] [planId]  Write or replace the decisions for a plan
  observations-write [options]        Write analyst observations for a plan
  spec-write [options]                Write the generated specification for a
                                      plan
  codex                               codex commands
  draft                               draft commands
  tools                               tools commands
  loom                                loom commands

input.command:
nsg commission-post --type brief --draft --title "Update Astrolabe sage instructions with value-laden razor and three codified defaults" --parent-id w-mo0v636y --codex nexus --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
# Update Astrolabe sage instructions with value-laden razor and three codified defaults

## Goal

Codify the findings from the planner-redesign umbrella (`c-mo1mq93f`) into the Astrolabe sage-writer and sage-analyst instructions. The changes apply a sharp razor for surfacing decisions to the patron, and three policy defaults for decisions that would otherwise surface. Expected effect: drop patron override rate from the ~3.7% baseline (see c-mo1yb8zb) toward ~2%, without expanding the set of surfaced decisions.

## Source Material

- **Razor for value-laden decisions** — concluded click `c-mo1yb8aj`
- **Codified policy defaults** — concluded click `c-mo1yb8nf`
- **Empirical baseline** — concluded click `c-mo1yb8zb` (3.7% override rate across 38 specs / ~945 decisions)
- **Umbrella** — `c-mo1mq93f` (Redesign Astrolabe planner for intent-and-constraints specs)
- **Shipped precedents** — `w-mo0ypqs4` (v1 task manifest), `w-mo0yqdyr` (v2 task-loop engine)

## The Razor

A planner decision surfaces to the patron only if it falls into one of five categories:

1. **Vocabulary/pattern establishment** — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow.
2. **Human-facing surface** — CLI text, error messages, agent personalities, doc phrasing, UX details.
3. **Scope boundary** — cutlines between commission and follow-up; 'should we also do X?' questions.
4. **Shape of persisted or inter-component data** — typed vs opaque, required vs optional, configured vs convention, when other components will consume the shape.
5. **Component responsibility boundaries** — who owns a behavior across engines/tools/apparatuses, when the decision sets a pattern for ownership.

All other decisions: pick and record. Planner uncertainty is not a surfacing signal — it is a cue to investigate more (read the codebase, check conventions, look at git history).

## The Three Codified Defaults

When resolving decisions that do not meet the razor, apply these defaults:

1. **Prefer removal to deprecation.** When refactoring, rip out the old path. No deprecation windows unless the patron explicitly asks.
2. **Prefer fail-loud to silent fallback.** Throw on missing input; no defaults-when-absent unless the absent case is a legitimate state.
3. **Extend the API at the right layer; don't route around it.** If the recommendation involves a workaround or 'the anima handles it via prompt,' default to adding the method/tool instead.

## Scope

- **sage-writer instructions** — update to apply the razor when composing the decisions section, and the three defaults when resolving decisions that don't surface.
- **sage-analyst instructions** — update to recognize the razor during decision identification. Decisions tagged with patron-audience must match one of the five criteria; otherwise the analyst picks and records.
- **sage-reader instructions** — no change expected.
- **Decision record format** — keep current format (question, options, selected, rationale, confidence, audience); no structural change.

## Out of Scope

- Preference-model / correction-graduation automation — dropped per `c-mo1mq9f6`
- Handoff notes for implement-loop — dropped per `c-mo1mqa1y`
- Tiered plumbing distinctions — scrapped per `c-mo1mq9qa`
- Atlas staleness work — separate cluster `c-mo1mqhes`

## Acceptance

- Sage-writer and sage-analyst instructions explicitly reflect the razor criteria and the three defaults.
- Verification: dispatch the planner on 3-5 matched commissions post-change; measure override rate vs the 3.7% baseline. Target: below 2.5%.
- Decisions the analyst tags as patron-audience must be explainable via one of the five razor criteria.
EOF
)"

- **Shipped precedents** — `w-mo0ypqs4` (v1 task manifest), `w-mo0yqdyr` (v2 task-loop engine)
- **Archived context** — `docs/archive/quests/w-mo0v636y-41c8aeff857f.json` (original planner-redesign quest body)

## The Razor

A planner decision surfaces to the patron only if it falls into one of five categories:

1. **Vocabulary/pattern establishment** — new guild terms, categorical distinctions, patterns other code will follow.

- **sage-reading-analyst instructions** — PRIMARY TARGET. Apply the razor when identifying decisions. A decision only gets tagged for patron audience if it matches one of the five razor criteria. Uncertainty about a non-razor decision is a cue to investigate more (extend the inventory scan, check codebase patterns), not to surface.
- **sage-analyst instructions** — apply the three codified defaults when picking among options for non-surfaced decisions. Also enforce the razor at audience-tagging: decisions with patron audience must cite the razor criterion that surfaces them.
- **sage-writer instructions** — render decisions faithfully; no razor logic here. Update to match any record-format adjustments from analyst changes.
- **sage-reader instructions** — no change expected. Reader produces the inventory; razor is downstream.

- Preference-model / correction-graduation automation — dropped per `c-mo1mq9f6`
- Handoff notes for implement-loop — dropped per `c-mo1mqa1y`
- Tiered plumbing distinctions — scrapped per `c-mo1mq9qa`
- Atlas staleness work — separate cluster `c-mo1mqhes`
- Decision record format changes beyond razor-citation (keep current fields: question, options, selected, rationale, confidence, audience)

## Acceptance

- All four sage instruction files updated as described above.
- sage-reading-analyst instructions explicitly list the five razor criteria and the investigation-before-surfacing rule.
- sage-analyst instructions explicitly list the three codified defaults and require razor-citation on patron-audience decisions.
- Verification: dispatch the planner on 3-5 matched commissions post-change; measure override rate vs the 3.7% baseline. Target: below 2.5%.
- Decisions the analyst tags as patron-audience must be explainable via one of the five razor criteria.
EOF
)"

Base directory for this skill: /workspace/nexus-mk2/.claude/skills/wrap-up

# Wrap-Up: Complete Work and Generate Session Summary

## Generate Summary

Generate a structured summary of the current session. This summary serves one audience:

**The Scribe** — as input for session notes and published material.

Session-to-session continuity is now carried by **clicks** (see Coco's agent file and the clicks skill). Before generating the wrap-up summary, conclude or park any clicks touched this session — the click tree is what the next Coco session will read for orientation, not this wrap-up file.

<expected-format>
# Session Summary

## What we did

- Bulleted list of concrete actions taken (files created/modified, decisions made, commits pushed). Reference specific files and commit hashes.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- What was decided, including explicit "we chose NOT to" decisions. Brief rationale for each.

## Next steps & open questions

- Concrete actions for the next session or for agents to pick up.
- Open questions which were raised but not answered

### Notable moments

Capture quotes, insights, or exchanges that stand out — either for system design or for the research/experiment narrative.

For each notable moment:
- Include **direct quotes from Sean** when available
- Flag **why it's notable** (e.g., "shifted the project's core abstraction", "surfaced a tension between X and Y", "revealed an assumption about how agents should work")
- Prioritize moments where the human changed direction, challenged an assumption, or articulated something new about the project's purpose

### Experiment observations

Flag anything relevant to active experiments. Omit this section if nothing applies.

<expected-format>

## Complete Work

After wrapping up, **ALWAYS**:

- Commit any changes you have made, if any
- Push main to the remote

## Reminders

Check for things Sean should be nudged about:

- **Ethnographer interview:** Check the most recent interview file in `experiments/ethnography/interviews/` (files named `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md`). If the most recent one is more than a day old — or if none exist yet — remind Sean to run an ethnographer session. Keep it light: *"It's been a couple days since your last ethnographer interview — might be a good time to check in."*

## Guidelines

- Keep the whole summary under ~500 words. Brevity is a feature.
- Do not editorialize beyond the "why it's notable" flags.
- Write the summary to `experiments/ethnography/session-notes/new/<timestamp>.md`. where `<timestamp>` is the current date and time formatted as `YYYY-MM-DDTHHMMSS.md` (use the actual current time — don't round to the nearest hour). Each session gets its own file — never overwrite a previous one. This file is for the ethnographer and must NOT be auto-deleted — it accumulates until the ethnographer reviews it.
- Commit all `session-notes`, and `experiments/data/transcripts/` changes to git and push those changes. The transcripts directory may contain files from concurrent sessions — commit whatever is there.
- In addition to writing the summary, share it in the chat for review and inclusion in session transcripts

    justification: sanctum

  - session: 7578529e-5d59-4d84-936f-124b248b357b
    date: 2026-04-16
    item: "H1 monolithic baseline rig-mo1wajm9 completed and falsified the cheap-mining projection. Monolithic ran Oculus spec in 77 turns / 12.5min / \\$7.07 billed — 0.29x turns, 0.28x wall, 0.38x cost of decomposed Rig 2. Review PASS (no required changes), seal fast-forward (vs decomposed's rebase-conflict seal failure). Auto-compaction observed at turn 63 (cache_read reset 166K→14K) — graceful, no quality impact. Projection was wrong by ~9x in the opposite direction; fatal assumption was 'same turn count'. Reality: decomposition inflates turns 3.5x via per-piece orientation tax. Committed artifact 2026-04-16-h1-monolithic-baseline.md + h1_monolithic.py script. Resumed and concluded monolithic-baseline click c-mo1w0g2n; concluded H1 parent click c-mo1vzvjp with the falsified finding. X010 root question reframed: 'under what (larger) conditions does staging pay off?' — H4 becomes primary."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 7578529e-5d59-4d84-936f-124b248b357b
    date: 2026-04-16
    item: "Side-by-side quality comparison of monolithic (merged to main as eb09729+be7cbc4) vs decomposed (stuck at draft-mo1o66gw) Oculus click-page implementations. Feature parity across all 25 decisions; both passed review. Mono index.html 1,043 lines vs decomposed 1,441 (+38%); mono has 58 tests vs decomposed 69 (+19%). Decomposed is more fragmented into small named helpers (renderLifecycleSection, renderLinksSection, etc.); coverage topics overlap almost entirely with a couple marginal extras (explicit missing-id breadcrumb path, non-string coercion). No quality premium observed to justify decomposition's 2.6x cost / 3.6x wall-time premium. Created and concluded policy click c-mo1xoggo: keep `implement` as default engine in guild.json; `implement-loop` available as per-commission opt-in."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

  - session: 7578529e-5d59-4d84-936f-124b248b357b
    date: 2026-04-16
    item: "X010 follow-ups: H3 and H2 children. Under the (parked) H3 parent c-mo1w039y, added+concluded c-mo1y5d22 recording the baseline's seal-reliability observation and the likely causal reframe (conflict probability tracks rig wall time, not engine choice directly). Under the live H2 parent c-mo1vzxhu, added c-mo1y8392 (design a quality instrument beyond pass/fail review — the H2 hypothesis needs one to be falsifiable) and c-mo1y846y (audit pre-vs-post-compaction output from the H1 baseline transcript). Ran the compaction audit: discovered the baseline session had TWO compactions (turn 11 and turn 63, not just 63); three-phase breakdown (A: 1-10 exploration, B: 11-62 main work incl commit 1, C: 63-77 test authoring incl commit 2). Phase C produced byte-perfect re-declared helpers matching Phase B's implementation — no drift, no hallucination, all 58 new tests passed first try. Post-compaction orientation tax was ~4 turns (vs ~8-10 per-piece orientation tax in decomposed data), reinforcing compaction as a cheaper handoff than decomposition. Added correction note to 2026-04-16-h1-monolithic-baseline.md + saved h2_compaction_audit.py in X010 scripts dir. Concluded c-mo1y846y with findings."
    commissionable: false
    justification: sanctum

input.content:
# Session Summary — 2026-04-16T21:01

## What we did

- Drilled into the planner-redesign umbrella (`c-mo1mq93f`); produced full dossier pulling the umbrella writ body, concluded/dropped clicks, transcripts, and scratch artifacts.
- Sean challenged the 4-tier decision model and the preference-model as not-MVP. I did the analytical work: enumerated candidate razor criteria, stress-tested against recent commissions, and proposed collapsing to a binary pick+record / surface split governed by a sharp razor.
- Mined all 38 `specs/*/decisions.yaml` files for patron overrides. Result: 35 overrides / ~945 decisions (**~3.7% baseline override rate**). 24 of 38 specs (63%) sailed through with zero overrides. Clustered overrides into six patterns; three of the six are codifiable policy preferences, not per-decision taste.
- Turned the analysis into 5 click changes under `c-mo1mq93f`: concluded the tier-model click; dropped the preference-model click; created+concluded three new clicks capturing the razor (`c-mo1yb8aj`), the three codified defaults (`c-mo1yb8nf`), and the empirical baseline (`c-mo1yb8zb`); cross-linked the razor to the atlas-staleness cluster.
- Dropped the handoff-notes trio (`c-mo1mqa1y` + children) as obsolete after X010 H1 findings.
- Concluded the umbrella click `c-mo1mq93f` with a six-pillar summary.
- Created draft brief writ **`w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63`** to commission the sage-instruction updates (scope expanded after Sean's prompt to cover all four sage roles — primary target is sage-reading-analyst where decisions actually surface).
- Added live click `c-mo1yf2y1` under `c-mo1itggx` noting that click-goal editability has surfaced as a need (immutability was explicit — not overriding, just logging).

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Scrap the 4-tier decision model.** Binary pick/surface + a razor is the MVP.
- **Drop the preference-model / correction-graduation automation.** Manual loop (patron reads records → updates instructions) suffices; ~40% of overrides are codifiable policy, not learnable taste.
- **Drop implement-loop handoff notes entirely.** X010 H1 reverted the default engine to single-session; decomposition's home is the parked lazy-decomp branch `c-mo1w08cs`.
- **Do NOT edit click umbrella goal text** (`c-mo1mq93f` still says "tiered decision model"). CLI has no edit command and Sean's immutability-by-design decision stands.
- **Conclude the umbrella even though operational follow-up remains.** The design is closed; implementation lives in the draft brief writ.

## Next steps & open questions

- Review and publish `w-mo1yp84y` when ready: `nsg writ show w-mo1yp84y && nsg writ publish w-mo1yp84y`.
- Re-measure override rate after sage updates ship; target below 2.5%.
- Open: whether click-goal editability is worth building; exhibit A is the stale `c-mo1mq93f` goal text.

### Notable moments

- **Sean reframing the whole tier question:** *"I'm not sure if the tiered model is the correct breakdown. I also feel like the 'self learning from past direction' is an aspirational goal, not an MVP one."* Cut both the tier structure and the self-learning ambition in one move. Notable because it shifted the design from a clever-mechanism model to a sharp-razor-plus-dumb-defaults model — exactly the kind of MVP discipline the autonomous-hopper goal depends on.
- **Sean redirecting scope at writ-creation time:** *"i guess its not really the writer only that needs to change, but also the reader-analyzer since that is where decisions surface."* Notable because it corrected my mental model of the Astrolabe pipeline — decisions surface during identification, not rendering. The razor needs to live upstream, not downstream.
- **Empirical pattern that stuck:** ~40% of patron overrides weren't value-judgments — they were Sean's already-expressed architectural taste (remove-over-deprecate, fail-loud, extend-API-not-route-around-it). Codifying taste is lower-cost than learning it.

### Experiment observations

- **X010 result used operationally.** The H1 falsification directly killed a sibling design line (handoff notes). Good example of experiment findings feeding live design decisions rather than sitting in artifacts.
- **Baseline measurement `c-mo1yb8zb` is a standing instrument.** Future comparisons depend on it being re-runnable — the mining was a simple yq extraction over `specs/*/decisions.yaml`, worth preserving as a script.

## Reminder for Sean

**Ethnographer interview is overdue.** Most recent was 2026-04-07 (9 days ago). Might be a good time to check in with the ethnographer.

#
# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
# picture of how work is routed in the system.
#
# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
#

content.text:
Committed and pushed as `90b7fc4`.

---

# Session Summary — 2026-04-16T21:01

## What we did

- Drilled into the planner-redesign umbrella (`c-mo1mq93f`); produced full dossier pulling the umbrella writ body, concluded/dropped clicks, transcripts, and scratch artifacts.
- Sean challenged the 4-tier decision model and the preference-model as not-MVP. I did the analytical work: enumerated candidate razor criteria, stress-tested against recent commissions, and proposed collapsing to a binary pick+record / surface split governed by a sharp razor.
- Mined all 38 `specs/*/decisions.yaml` files for patron overrides: 35 overrides / ~945 decisions (**~3.7% baseline override rate**). 24 of 38 specs (63%) sailed through clean. Six pattern clusters; three are codifiable policy, not per-decision taste.
- Turned the analysis into 5 click changes under `c-mo1mq93f`: concluded the tier-model click; dropped the preference-model click; created+concluded three new clicks capturing the razor (`c-mo1yb8aj`), three codified defaults (`c-mo1yb8nf`), and empirical baseline (`c-mo1yb8zb`); cross-linked the razor to the atlas-staleness cluster.
- Dropped handoff-notes trio (`c-mo1mqa1y` + children) as obsolete after X010 H1 findings.
- Concluded the umbrella `c-mo1mq93f` with a six-pillar summary.
- Created draft brief writ **`w-mo1yp84y-7bf521a80d63`** to commission the sage-instruction updates; scope expanded after Sean's redirect to cover all four sage roles (sage-reading-analyst is primary target since decisions surface there).
- Added live click `c-mo1yf2y1` under `c-mo1itggx` noting click-goal editability has surfaced as a need.

## Decisions made and/or deferred

- **Scrap the 4-tier model.** Binary pick/surface + razor is the MVP.
- **Drop correction-graduation automation.** Manual loop suffices; ~40% of overrides are codifiable policy, not learnable taste.
- **Drop implement-loop handoff notes entirely.** X010 H1 reverted default to single-session; decomposition's home is the parked lazy-decomp branch `c-mo1w08cs`.
- **Do NOT edit click umbrella goal text.** CLI has no edit command; immutability-by-design preserved.
- **Conclude umbrella even though operational follow-up remains.** Implementation lives in the draft brief writ.

## Next steps & open questions

- Review and publish `w-mo1yp84y` when ready.
- Re-measure override rate after sage updates ship; target below 2.5%.
- Open: whether click-goal editability is worth building (exhibit A: the stale `c-mo1mq93f` goal text).

### Notable moments

- **Sean reframing the whole tier question:** *"I'm not sure if the tiered model is the correct breakdown. I also feel like the 'self learning from past direction' is an aspirational goal, not an MVP one."* Notable because it shifted the design from a clever-mechanism model to a sharp-razor-plus-dumb-defaults model — exactly the MVP discipline the autonomous-hopper goal depends on.
- **Sean redirecting scope at writ-creation time:** *"i guess its not really the writer only that needs to change, but also the reader-analyzer since that is where decisions surface."* Corrected my mental model of the Astrolabe pipeline — the razor needs to live at identification, not rendering.
- **Empirical pattern that stuck:** ~40% of patron overrides weren't value-judgments — they were Sean's already-expressed architectural taste (remove-over-deprecate, fail-loud, extend-API-not-route-around-it). Codifying taste is lower-cost than learning it.

### Experiment observations

- **X010 result used operationally.** H1 falsification directly killed a sibling design line (handoff notes). Good example of experiment findings feeding live design decisions.
- **Baseline measurement `c-mo1yb8zb` is a standing instrument.** Future comparisons depend on it being re-runnable — simple yq extraction, worth preserving as a script.

---

## Reminder

**Ethnographer interview is overdue** — most recent was 2026-04-07, 9 days ago. Might be a good time to check in with the ethnographer.
